title: Programmable superconducting diode from nematic domain control in FeS
authors: R. D. H. Hinlopen, C. Putzke, L. Holeschovsky, R. Nicholls, F. Ronning, E. D. Bauer, N. E. Hussey, P. J. W. Moll
abstract: The superconducting diode effect (SDE) allows polarity-dependent critical currents when time-reversal and current-inverting spatial symmetries are broken. Superconducting diodes show promise for applications, but inversion asymmetry is usually encoded in sample geometry or non-centrosymmetric crystals, rendering them static circuit elements. Here we demonstrate a programmable superconducting diode whose functionality is encoded in correlated electronic domains. We use the nematic superconductor FeSe as a platform and report a large intrinsic SDE with efficiencies up to $η\sim 75\%$ due to vortices interacting with nematic twin boundaries. The domain wall configuration thus encodes the SDE of the device. Through intense microsecond current pulses to quench the nematic order at rates exceeding $10^7$~K/s, we modify the domain pattern and control the polarity and strength of the SDE. These results establish a new paradigm in which superconducting circuit elements can be programmed through patterns imprinted into correlated electronic states.
summary: This paper presents a method to create a programmable superconducting diode using the electronic nematicity of FeSe. By using rapid current pulses to quench the material, the researchers can manipulate the pattern of nematic domain walls, effectively 'writing' the diode's polarity and strength. This moves superconducting diode technology from static, fixed-geometry components to reconfigurable, programmable circuit elements. The effect is highly efficient and remains robust even in very high magnetic fields.
main_problem: Traditional superconducting diodes are static circuit elements because their asymmetry is fixed by crystal geometry or structure. The goal is to create a programmable superconducting diode where the polarity and strength can be tuned.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated a programmable superconducting diode in FeSe where the diode effect is controlled by manipulating nematic twin domain patterns via rapid thermal quenching. They achieved high diode efficiencies of up to 75% and showed that the device state can be 'written' using current pulses.
method: The team used cryogenic Focused Ion Beam (FIB) milling to create symmetric FeSe microstructures and applied intense microsecond current pulses to quench nematic order at rates exceeding 10^7 K/s. Transport measurements, including angle-dependent magnetotransport and pulsed I-V curves, were used to characterize the diode efficiency and domain states.
model_or_system: The physical platform is FeSe, an unconventional, layered, electronic-nematic superconductor. The system relies on the interaction between superconducting vortices and nematic twin boundaries to break inversion symmetry.
key_observables: Superconducting diode efficiency (eta), critical currents (Ic+ and Ic-), resistivity, and nematic transition temperature (Ts).
important_parameters: Quench rates (up to 12 MK/s), magnetic field strength (up to 12-16 T), temperature (near Tc and Ts), and current pulse width (microsecond scale).
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the observed asymmetry is intrinsic to the domain wall interactions rather than structural defects introduced by the FIB process, which they verify via XPS/EDX.
figures_summary: Fig 1 illustrates the quenching process and transport signatures; Fig 3 demonstrates the programming protocol and the ability to cycle through different diode states; Fig 4 shows the dependence of the SDE on the magnetic field angle and the underlying symmetry mechanism.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of static diodes, describes the fabrication of FeSe microstructures, details the thermal quenching method for programming, presents the results of the high-efficiency diode effect, and concludes with the physical mechanism involving vortex-domain wall interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Quenching the nematic order in FeSe. a Adiabatically cooling FeSe microstructures results in a macro-domained electronic nematic state. By contrast, heat quenching FeSe welded to a substrate at 10 MK/s stabilizes a nano-domained state through inhomogeneous thermal expansion. In this fashion, quench rates can be used to control the density and size of the nematic domains. 4-leaf flower and peanut shapes indicate electron pocket shape and orientation [11, 12] b Cryogenic FIB- manufactured microstructures used in this study to measure c-axis transport. The first two devices are made out of a single lamella, the left labeled device 1, each with cross section ≈50 µm2. The third device is...

Fig. 2 Superconducting diode effect at twin pinning directions a Single-axis in-plane rota- tion of the magnetic field while in the flux flow regime for the out-of-plane resistivity. Vortex pinning is observed in narrow angle ranges along a/b directions when the magnetic field is along the surface of the structure (marked Geo), while stronger pinning is observed when the magnetic field is near the (110) direction along the nematic twin domain walls. Inset: Magnetic field and current orientation in a macrodomained sample. b At lower temperatures in the superconducting state, a large supercon- ducting diode effect is observed when the magnetic field is near the twin boundary direction. Fig. 3 Programming the SDE in FeSe. a Pulse shape for a comparatively slow anneal within 1ms from room temperature, cooling through Ts at 1.3 MK/s. The temperature profile is estimated by converting the time-dependent resistance using the cooldown curve. Uncertainty of order 10 % exists due to thermal inhomogeneity of the sample, most evident near the maximum in R(T) at 250 K. b Rapid and hot quench from nearly room temperature at 10 MK/s. c Rapid but colder quench from Tmax < 200 K at similar quench rate. d We cycle through the pulses and alternate each one with square-pulse IV curves to write and read the SDE. The curves are colored based on the directly preceding pulse type. The 35 IV...

Fig. 4 Twin boundary origin and leaning vortices. a Typical angle scan of the SDE showing quasi-Lorentzian critical current enhancement with shift between current polarities. Measured using the lock-in method at low currents. The SDE is inverted when the magnetic field is rotated through the twin domain boundary direction at ϕ = 45◦and diminishes after ∼10◦misalignment in ϕ direction at 0.3 T. b Angle dependence of the SDE using square-pulse IV characteristics at 12 T showing the persistence of the diode effect.
